dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unknown problem/person down at a bus stop in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Carlos, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened here today?
There's a man in his seventies lying on the ground. He appears confused.
Can you open your eyes for me? What is your name?
What is the year and month today? Can you help us with that?
Please raise your arms for me. Show me you can follow directions.
Squeeze my finger firmly. Let me assess motor coordination.
Your airway is clear and you're maintaining it well. No signs of blockage.
Blood pressure is 139 over 77. That's within normal limits.
Oxygen saturation is at 96%. No signs of cyanosis.
You're breathing at 20 per minute. Rate and depth appear normal.
Respirations are regular and even in depth.
Heart rate is 92, strong and consistent.
Radial and pedal pulses are strong. Your skin is warm and dry, though you have some sweating under your arms.
FAST showed no facial droop or drift. You had trouble repeating the phrase "you can't teach an old dog new tricks."
There's a 3x5cm skin tear at your right elbow. No other rashes or bruises noted.
Skin is normal in color except for some sweat. No bony deformities or bruises on the legs or arms.
We are using capnography for continuous respiratory monitoring as a precaution.
Left pupil is 1mm, non-reactive. Right is 2mm, minimally reactive. Have you ever had unequal pupils before?
Any pain or tenderness in your neck or back at the moment?
You mentioned feeling off-balance when standing. Did that happen when you were getting up?
Yes, I felt a bit off-balance but no sharp pain.
Let me clarify the events. What do you remember happening?
How long had you been on the ground when we arrived?
At first, I thought maybe a car hit me in the back. Then I realized it was a motorcycle.
So you at first believed a car hit you, then corrected to a motorcycle impact?
Yes. It was a motorcycle - nothing else.
After the impact, did you feel numbness, tingling, or pain anywhere?
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now?
I'd say about a 2. No sharp discomfort.
Did you feel lightheaded or unsteady when responders helped you to your feet?
Just a little unsteady. I didn't fall again.
Any prior medical conditions or episodes like this?
Are you currently taking any medications or pain relievers?
Any allergies to medications, foods, or other substances?
I don't take any medications. No known allergies.
Have you had any foods, drinks, or alcohol in the last day?
Have you used any drugs or consumed pain medications recently?
Are you on any blood thinners or anticoagulants?
Not that I recall, though I may have had a few sips of alcohol earlier.
We're measuring your blood sugar now for the safety assessment.
Connecting you to a 4-lead ECG as part of standard protocol.
Your blood sugar is 90. That's within a normal range.
EKG showed left bundle branch block, first-degree AV block, and signs of left ventricular hypertrophy.
No signs of spinal injury. Any back or neck pain at the moment?
We'll first support you to a bench to begin transport preparations.
We'll help you pivot to the stretcher with care. Stay with us.
The hospital has been informed. A trauma team is waiting at [COMPANY_NAME] for further care.
You are now secured with five-point restraints to ensure your safety during the ride.
Second 12-lead EKG shows sinus rhythm and left lateral T-wave changes.
Blood pressure is now 116/71 - still within a normal range.
Breathing is now at 16 per minute. Regular in effort and pattern.
Heart rate has dropped a bit to 79 - still strong and regular.
Has your pain level changed since earlier? Still on a 2?
Some final safety checks before moving. No further treatment is needed at this time.
We're now preparing to transport to [COMPANY_NAME] under protocol 3-15.
You'll be comfortable and secure during the drive to the hospital.
Vitals and documentation are complete. Care will be taken over at the hospital.
Trauma team is ready at [COMPANY_NAME]. We'll handle the next steps.
Field impression is altered mental status and EKG abnormalities per ePCR.
Paramedics have taken over. You'll be in good hands at the hospital.
We've started an IV in your arm to deliver fluids if necessary.
Your gait was unsteady when standing. You had trouble maintaining balance.
Diaphoresis noted at the axillas. It's mild and not concerning.
Skin color is good with no noted pallor or jaundice.
IV access is in a leg vein. Site is patent and secure.
